title: Exercises: Qubit Modalities
description: Practice comparing qubit technologies through quantitative analysis and simulation

exercises.title: Quality Factor Calculator
exercises.description: Implement a function to calculate and compare the quality factors (T2/t_gate) for different qubit modalities. The quality factor indicates how many gate operations can be performed within one coherence time.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def calculate_quality_factors(modality_data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate quality factors for different qubit modalities.
    
    Args:
        modality_data: Dictionary with modality names as keys and 
                      dictionaries containing 'T2', 't_gate_1q', 't_gate_2q' as values
                      (all times in seconds)
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with modality names as keys and dictionaries containing
        'quality_1q' and 'quality_2q' as values
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test data (times in seconds)
test_data = {
    'Superconducting': {'T2': 100e-6, 't_gate_1q': 20e-9, 't_gate_2q': 100e-9},
    'Trapped Ion': {'T2': 10, 't_gate_1q': 1e-6, 't_gate_2q': 100e-6},
    'Neutral Atom': {'T2': 1, 't_gate_1q': 1e-6, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6},
    'Spin Qubit': {'T2': 1e-3, 't_gate_1q': 100e-9, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6}
}

results = calculate_quality_factors(test_data)
print("Quality Factors (T2/t_gate):")
print("=" * 50)
for modality, factors in results.items():
    print(f"{modality:20s}: 1Q = {factors['quality_1q']:.2e}, 2Q = {factors['quality_2q']:.2e}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def calculate_quality_factors(modality_data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate quality factors for different qubit modalities.
    
    Args:
        modality_data: Dictionary with modality names as keys and 
                      dictionaries containing 'T2', 't_gate_1q', 't_gate_2q' as values
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with quality factors for each modality
    """
    results = {}
    
    for modality, data in modality_data.items():
        T2 = data['T2']
        t_1q = data['t_gate_1q']
        t_2q = data['t_gate_2q']
        
        quality_1q = T2 / t_1q
        quality_2q = T2 / t_2q
        
        results[modality] = {
            'quality_1q': quality_1q,
            'quality_2q': quality_2q,
            'T2': T2,
            't_gate_1q': t_1q,
            't_gate_2q': t_2q
        }
    
    return results

def plot_quality_comparison(results: dict):
    """Create a bar chart comparing quality factors."""
    modalities = list(results.keys())
    q1 = [np.log10(results[m]['quality_1q']) for m in modalities]
    q2 = [np.log10(results[m]['quality_2q']) for m in modalities]
    
    x = np.arange(len(modalities))
    width = 0.35
    
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    bars1 = ax.bar(x - width/2, q1, width, label='1-Qubit Gates', color='steelblue')
    bars2 = ax.bar(x + width/2, q2, width, label='2-Qubit Gates', color='coral')
    
    ax.set_ylabel('log10(Quality Factor)')
    ax.set_title('Qubit Modality Quality Factors (T2/t_gate)')
    ax.set_xticks(x)
    ax.set_xticklabels(modalities, rotation=45, ha='right')
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    
    # Add threshold line for error correction
    ax.axhline(y=4, color='green', linestyle='--', label='Typical QEC threshold')
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Test data
test_data = {
    'Superconducting': {'T2': 100e-6, 't_gate_1q': 20e-9, 't_gate_2q': 100e-9},
    'Trapped Ion': {'T2': 10, 't_gate_1q': 1e-6, 't_gate_2q': 100e-6},
    'Neutral Atom': {'T2': 1, 't_gate_1q': 1e-6, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6},
    'Spin Qubit': {'T2': 1e-3, 't_gate_1q': 100e-9, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6}
}

results = calculate_quality_factors(test_data)
print("Quality Factors (T2/t_gate):")
print("=" * 50)
for modality, factors in results.items():
    print(f"{modality:20s}: 1Q = {factors['quality_1q']:.2e}, 2Q = {factors['quality_2q']:.2e}")

print("\nInterpretation:")
print("Quality factor indicates max gate operations per coherence time.")
print("Higher is better - need ~10^4 for fault-tolerant QEC.")

plot_quality_comparison(results)
hints: Quality factor is simply the ratio T2 / gate_time
Calculate separately for single-qubit and two-qubit gates
Use logarithmic scale for visualization due to large range

exercises.title: Modality Selection Analysis
exercises.description: For a quantum algorithm requiring 10,000 two-qubit gates per qubit, analyze which modalities can complete the circuit within their coherence time. Calculate the minimum required gate fidelity for each modality to achieve overall circuit fidelity > 50%.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def analyze_circuit_feasibility(n_gates: int, modality_params: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Analyze whether each modality can execute a circuit with n_gates 2-qubit gates.
    
    Args:
        n_gates: Number of two-qubit gates in the circuit
        modality_params: Dictionary with T2, t_gate_2q, gate_fidelity_2q for each modality
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with analysis results for each modality including:
        - can_complete: Boolean, whether circuit fits in coherence time
        - circuit_time: Total circuit execution time
        - time_ratio: circuit_time / T2
        - expected_fidelity: (gate_fidelity)^n_gates accounting for decoherence
        - min_fidelity_for_50pct: Minimum gate fidelity needed for 50% circuit fidelity
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test parameters
params = {
    'Superconducting': {'T2': 100e-6, 't_gate_2q': 100e-9, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.995},
    'Trapped Ion': {'T2': 10, 't_gate_2q': 100e-6, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.995},
    'Neutral Atom': {'T2': 1, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.99}
}

results = analyze_circuit_feasibility(10000, params)
print(f"Analysis for circuit with 10,000 two-qubit gates:")
print("=" * 60)
for mod, data in results.items():
    print(f"\n{mod}:")
    for key, value in data.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def analyze_circuit_feasibility(n_gates: int, modality_params: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Analyze whether each modality can execute a circuit with n_gates 2-qubit gates.
    """
    results = {}
    
    for modality, params in modality_params.items():
        T2 = params['T2']
        t_gate = params['t_gate_2q']
        fidelity = params['gate_fidelity_2q']
        
        # Total circuit time
        circuit_time = n_gates * t_gate
        
        # Can circuit complete within coherence time?
        time_ratio = circuit_time / T2
        can_complete = time_ratio < 1.0
        
        # Expected fidelity from gate errors alone
        fidelity_from_gates = fidelity ** n_gates
        
        # Decoherence contribution (exponential decay)
        decoherence_factor = np.exp(-circuit_time / T2)
        
        # Combined fidelity
        expected_fidelity = fidelity_from_gates * decoherence_factor
        
        # Minimum fidelity for 50% circuit fidelity
        # F_circuit = F_gate^n * exp(-t/T2) > 0.5
        # F_gate^n > 0.5 / exp(-t/T2)
        # n * log(F_gate) > log(0.5) + t/T2
        # F_gate > exp((log(0.5) + t/T2) / n)
        min_fidelity = np.exp((np.log(0.5) + circuit_time/T2) / n_gates)
        min_fidelity = min(min_fidelity, 0.9999)  # Cap at realistic value
        
        # Express as required error rate
        max_error_rate = 1 - min_fidelity
        
        results[modality] = {
            'can_complete': can_complete,
            'circuit_time_ms': circuit_time * 1000,
            'T2_ms': T2 * 1000,
            'time_ratio': f"{time_ratio:.2%}",
            'fidelity_from_gates': f"{fidelity_from_gates:.2e}",
            'decoherence_factor': f"{decoherence_factor:.4f}",
            'expected_fidelity': f"{expected_fidelity:.2e}",
            'min_fidelity_for_50pct': f"{min_fidelity:.6f}",
            'max_error_rate': f"{max_error_rate:.2e}"
        }
    
    return results

# Test parameters
params = {
    'Superconducting': {'T2': 100e-6, 't_gate_2q': 100e-9, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.995},
    'Trapped Ion': {'T2': 10, 't_gate_2q': 100e-6, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.995},
    'Neutral Atom': {'T2': 1, 't_gate_2q': 1e-6, 'gate_fidelity_2q': 0.99}
}

results = analyze_circuit_feasibility(10000, params)
print(f"Analysis for circuit with 10,000 two-qubit gates:")
print("=" * 70)
for mod, data in results.items():
    print(f"\n{mod}:")
    for key, value in data.items():
        print(f"  {key:25s}: {value}")

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("CONCLUSIONS:")
print("- Superconducting: Circuit time (1ms) > T2 (0.1ms) - FAILS")
print("- Trapped Ion: Circuit time (1s) << T2 (10s) - PASSES but slow")
print("- Neutral Atom: Circuit time (10ms) << T2 (1s) - PASSES")
print("\nFor 50% success rate with 10,000 gates:")
print("Minimum gate fidelity required: ~99.993% (error rate < 7e-5)")
hints: Circuit time = number of gates * gate time
Time ratio = circuit_time / T2 must be < 1 for feasibility
Total fidelity combines gate errors and decoherence: F = F_gate^n * exp(-t/T2)
For minimum fidelity, solve F^n * exp(-t/T2) = 0.5

exercises.title: Rydberg Blockade Radius Calculator
exercises.description: Calculate the Rydberg blockade radius for neutral atom qubits as a function of principal quantum number n and Rabi frequency. The blockade radius determines the minimum spacing for independent Rydberg excitation.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def rydberg_blockade_radius(n: int, Omega_MHz: float, species: str = 'Rb87') -> dict:
    """
    Calculate Rydberg blockade radius and related parameters.
    
    The van der Waals coefficient scales as C6 ~ n^11
    The blockade radius is R_b = (C6 / (hbar * Omega))^(1/6)
    
    Args:
        n: Principal quantum number (typically 50-100)
        Omega_MHz: Rabi frequency in MHz
        species: Atomic species ('Rb87', 'Cs133')
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with:
        - C6_GHz_um6: Van der Waals coefficient in GHz*um^6
        - R_b_um: Blockade radius in micrometers
        - interaction_at_5um_MHz: Interaction strength at 5 um spacing
        - max_atoms_in_blockade: Approximate atoms that fit in blockade sphere
    """
    # Your code here
    # Reference: For Rb87, C6 ~ 2.4e9 * (n/70)^11 GHz*um^6
    pass

# Calculate for different n values
print("Rydberg Blockade Analysis for Rb87:")
print("=" * 60)
for n in [50, 70, 100]:
    result = rydberg_blockade_radius(n, Omega_MHz=1.0)
    print(f"\nn = {n}:")
    for key, value in result.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def rydberg_blockade_radius(n: int, Omega_MHz: float, species: str = 'Rb87') -> dict:
    """
    Calculate Rydberg blockade radius and related parameters.
    
    Args:
        n: Principal quantum number (typically 50-100)
        Omega_MHz: Rabi frequency in MHz  
        species: Atomic species ('Rb87', 'Cs133')
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with Rydberg interaction parameters
    """
    # Van der Waals coefficient reference values (in GHz * um^6)
    # These are approximate for nS states
    C6_ref = {
        'Rb87': 2.4e9,   # at n=70
        'Cs133': 3.2e9   # at n=70
    }
    n_ref = 70
    
    # C6 scales as n^11
    C6_GHz_um6 = C6_ref.get(species, 2.4e9) * (n / n_ref) ** 11
    
    # Convert Omega to GHz
    Omega_GHz = Omega_MHz / 1000
    
    # Blockade radius: R_b = (C6 / Omega)^(1/6)
    # Units: (GHz * um^6 / GHz)^(1/6) = um
    R_b_um = (C6_GHz_um6 / Omega_GHz) ** (1/6)
    
    # Interaction strength at typical 5 um spacing
    R_typical = 5.0  # um
    V_5um_GHz = C6_GHz_um6 / (R_typical ** 6)
    V_5um_MHz = V_5um_GHz * 1000
    
    # Approximate number of atoms that fit in blockade sphere
    # Assuming typical 3D array with 5 um spacing
    atom_spacing = 5.0  # um
    atoms_per_dimension = int(2 * R_b_um / atom_spacing)
    max_atoms = atoms_per_dimension ** 3 if atoms_per_dimension > 0 else 1
    
    # Gate time estimate (pi pulse with blockade)
    gate_time_ns = 1000 / Omega_MHz  # Rough estimate: pi/Omega
    
    return {
        'C6_GHz_um6': f"{C6_GHz_um6:.2e}",
        'R_b_um': f"{R_b_um:.2f}",
        'interaction_at_5um_MHz': f"{V_5um_MHz:.1f}",
        'blockade_ratio': f"{V_5um_MHz/Omega_MHz:.1f}x Omega",
        'max_atoms_in_blockade': max_atoms,
        'estimated_gate_time_ns': f"{gate_time_ns:.0f}"
    }

def plot_blockade_scaling(Omega_MHz: float = 1.0):
    """Plot blockade radius vs principal quantum number."""
    n_values = np.arange(40, 120, 5)
    
    # Calculate for different species
    R_b_Rb = []
    C6_Rb = []
    
    for n in n_values:
        result = rydberg_blockade_radius(n, Omega_MHz, 'Rb87')
        R_b_Rb.append(float(result['R_b_um']))
        C6_Rb.append(float(result['C6_GHz_um6'].replace('e+', 'e')))
    
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
    
    # Blockade radius plot
    ax1.plot(n_values, R_b_Rb, 'b-o', linewidth=2, markersize=4)
    ax1.set_xlabel('Principal Quantum Number n')
    ax1.set_ylabel('Blockade Radius (um)')
    ax1.set_title(f'Rydberg Blockade Radius (Omega = {Omega_MHz} MHz)')
    ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    ax1.axhline(y=5, color='r', linestyle='--', label='Typical atom spacing')
    ax1.legend()
    
    # C6 scaling plot (log scale)
    ax2.semilogy(n_values, C6_Rb, 'g-o', linewidth=2, markersize=4)
    ax2.set_xlabel('Principal Quantum Number n')
    ax2.set_ylabel('C6 (GHz * um^6)')
    ax2.set_title('Van der Waals Coefficient Scaling (C6 ~ n^11)')
    ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Calculate for different n values
print("Rydberg Blockade Analysis for Rb87:")
print("=" * 60)
for n in [50, 70, 100]:
    result = rydberg_blockade_radius(n, Omega_MHz=1.0)
    print(f"\nn = {n}:")
    for key, value in result.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("Key insights:")
print("- C6 scales dramatically with n (n^11)")
print("- Higher n gives larger blockade radius but more sensitivity to fields")
print("- Typical experiments use n = 60-80 for balance")
print("- Need V >> Omega for strong blockade (ratio > 10 preferred)")

# Uncomment to generate plot:
# plot_blockade_scaling()
hints: C6 coefficient scales as n^11 - this is a very strong scaling
Strong blockade requires interaction >> Rabi frequency
Reference value: For Rb87 at n=70, C6 ~ 2.4e9 GHz*um^6

testCases.expected: R_b ~ 30-50 um (much larger due to n^11 scaling)

exercises.title: Technology Roadmap Projection
exercises.description: Using historical improvement rates, project the capabilities of different qubit modalities to 2030. Consider coherence times, gate fidelities, and qubit counts. Analyze when each modality might reach fault-tolerant thresholds.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime

def project_modality_progress(historical_data: dict, target_year: int = 2030) -> dict:
    """
    Project future capabilities based on historical improvement rates.
    
    Args:
        historical_data: Dictionary with modality names, containing:
            - 'years': List of years with data points
            - 'qubit_count': Corresponding qubit counts
            - 'gate_fidelity': Two-qubit gate fidelities (as decimals)
            - 'T2_us': Coherence times in microseconds
        target_year: Year to project to
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with projections and analysis
    """
    # Your code here
    # Use exponential/linear fitting as appropriate
    # Consider saturation effects for fidelity (can't exceed 1)
    pass

# Historical data (simplified)
historical = {
    'Superconducting': {
        'years': [2016, 2019, 2022, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [5, 53, 433, 1121],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.98, 0.995, 0.998, 0.999],
        'T2_us': [20, 50, 100, 150]
    },
    'Trapped Ion': {
        'years': [2016, 2019, 2022, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [5, 11, 32, 56],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.99, 0.995, 0.998, 0.999],
        'T2_us': [1e6, 2e6, 5e6, 1e7]
    },
    'Neutral Atom': {
        'years': [2018, 2021, 2023, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [50, 256, 1200, 6100],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.95, 0.97, 0.99, 0.995],
        'T2_us': [1e5, 5e5, 1e6, 2e6]
    }
}

projections = project_modality_progress(historical, 2030)
for mod, proj in projections.items():
    print(f"\n{mod} - 2030 Projection:")
    for key, value in proj.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from datetime import datetime

def exponential_model(x, a, b):
    """Exponential growth: y = a * exp(b * x)"""
    return a * np.exp(b * x)

def logistic_model(x, L, k, x0):
    """Logistic growth with saturation: y = L / (1 + exp(-k*(x-x0)))"""
    return L / (1 + np.exp(-k * (x - x0)))

def project_modality_progress(historical_data: dict, target_year: int = 2030) -> dict:
    """
    Project future capabilities based on historical improvement rates.
    """
    current_year = 2024
    projections = {}
    
    for modality, data in historical_data.items():
        years = np.array(data['years']) - data['years'][0]  # Normalize
        target_x = target_year - data['years'][0]
        
        # Project qubit count (exponential growth)
        qubit_counts = np.array(data['qubit_count'])
        try:
            popt_q, _ = curve_fit(exponential_model, years, qubit_counts, 
                                  p0=[qubit_counts[0], 0.5], maxfev=5000)
            projected_qubits = int(exponential_model(target_x, *popt_q))
            doubling_time = np.log(2) / popt_q[1]
        except:
            # Fallback: simple extrapolation
            growth_rate = (qubit_counts[-1] / qubit_counts[0]) ** (1/(years[-1] - years[0]))
            projected_qubits = int(qubit_counts[-1] * growth_rate ** (target_year - data['years'][-1]))
            doubling_time = np.log(2) / np.log(growth_rate)
        
        # Project fidelity (logistic toward 0.9999)
        fidelities = np.array(data['gate_fidelity'])
        # Transform to error rate for better fitting
        error_rates = 1 - fidelities
        # Assume exponential improvement in error rate
        try:
            popt_e, _ = curve_fit(exponential_model, years, error_rates,
                                  p0=[error_rates[0], -0.3], maxfev=5000)
            projected_error = max(exponential_model(target_x, *popt_e), 1e-5)  # Floor at 10^-5
            projected_fidelity = 1 - projected_error
        except:
            # Fallback
            projected_fidelity = min(0.9999, fidelities[-1] + 0.001 * (target_year - data['years'][-1]))
            projected_error = 1 - projected_fidelity
        
        # Project T2 (exponential improvement)
        T2_values = np.array(data['T2_us'])
        try:
            popt_t, _ = curve_fit(exponential_model, years, T2_values,
                                  p0=[T2_values[0], 0.2], maxfev=5000)
            projected_T2 = exponential_model(target_x, *popt_t)
        except:
            improvement_rate = (T2_values[-1] / T2_values[0]) ** (1/(years[-1] - years[0]))
            projected_T2 = T2_values[-1] * improvement_rate ** (target_year - data['years'][-1])
        
        # Assess fault tolerance readiness
        # Threshold typically requires ~99.9% fidelity and ~10^4 quality factor
        ft_ready = projected_fidelity > 0.999 and projected_qubits > 1000
        
        projections[modality] = {
            'projected_qubits_2030': f"{projected_qubits:,}",
            'qubit_doubling_time_years': f"{doubling_time:.1f}",
            'projected_2q_fidelity': f"{projected_fidelity:.5f}",
            'projected_error_rate': f"{projected_error:.2e}",
            'projected_T2_ms': f"{projected_T2/1000:.1f}",
            'fault_tolerant_ready': ft_ready,
            'confidence': 'Medium - based on exponential extrapolation'
        }
    
    return projections

def plot_projections(historical_data: dict, projections: dict):
    """Create visualization of historical data and projections."""
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    
    colors = {'Superconducting': 'blue', 'Trapped Ion': 'green', 'Neutral Atom': 'orange'}
    
    for mod, data in historical_data.items():
        c = colors[mod]
        
        # Qubit count
        axes[0].semilogy(data['years'], data['qubit_count'], 'o-', color=c, label=mod)
        proj_q = int(projections[mod]['projected_qubits_2030'].replace(',', ''))
        axes[0].scatter([2030], [proj_q], marker='*', s=200, color=c)
        
        # Fidelity
        axes[1].plot(data['years'], data['gate_fidelity'], 'o-', color=c, label=mod)
        proj_f = float(projections[mod]['projected_2q_fidelity'])
        axes[1].scatter([2030], [proj_f], marker='*', s=200, color=c)
        
        # T2
        axes[2].semilogy(data['years'], np.array(data['T2_us'])/1000, 'o-', color=c, label=mod)
    
    axes[0].set_xlabel('Year')
    axes[0].set_ylabel('Qubit Count')
    axes[0].set_title('Qubit Scaling')
    axes[0].legend()
    axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    
    axes[1].set_xlabel('Year')
    axes[1].set_ylabel('2Q Gate Fidelity')
    axes[1].set_title('Gate Fidelity Improvement')
    axes[1].axhline(y=0.999, color='red', linestyle='--', label='FT threshold')
    axes[1].legend()
    axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    
    axes[2].set_xlabel('Year')
    axes[2].set_ylabel('T2 (ms)')
    axes[2].set_title('Coherence Time')
    axes[2].legend()
    axes[2].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Historical data
historical = {
    'Superconducting': {
        'years': [2016, 2019, 2022, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [5, 53, 433, 1121],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.98, 0.995, 0.998, 0.999],
        'T2_us': [20, 50, 100, 150]
    },
    'Trapped Ion': {
        'years': [2016, 2019, 2022, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [5, 11, 32, 56],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.99, 0.995, 0.998, 0.999],
        'T2_us': [1e6, 2e6, 5e6, 1e7]
    },
    'Neutral Atom': {
        'years': [2018, 2021, 2023, 2024],
        'qubit_count': [50, 256, 1200, 6100],
        'gate_fidelity': [0.95, 0.97, 0.99, 0.995],
        'T2_us': [1e5, 5e5, 1e6, 2e6]
    }
}

projections = project_modality_progress(historical, 2030)
print("2030 Projections for Quantum Hardware Modalities")
print("=" * 60)
for mod, proj in projections.items():
    print(f"\n{mod}:")
    for key, value in proj.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("ANALYSIS SUMMARY:")
print("- Superconducting: Strong qubit scaling, approaching FT fidelities")
print("- Trapped Ion: Best fidelities, but slower qubit scaling")
print("- Neutral Atom: Fastest qubit growth, fidelity improving rapidly")
print("\nCaveats: Projections assume continued exponential trends")
print("Physical limits may cause saturation earlier than projected")
hints: Qubit counts typically follow exponential growth
Gate fidelity improvements show diminishing returns approaching 1
Use error rate (1-fidelity) for better curve fitting
Consider physical limits: fidelity can't exceed 1, coherence has fundamental limits
